title: Child's own preference in custody disputes - at what age does it matter
p1:
A child saying 'I want to live with mom or dad' isn't automatically decisive in court. Many parents are surprised by what actually happens next.

What people assume is rarely how the law works.
p2: A common misconception is that courts follow a fixed age—like 10 or 12—when a child's preference becomes binding. In reality, there is no universal age cutoff. The focus is on how mature and independent the child's opinion is, not just their birthday.
p3: Courts decide custody based on the 'best interests of the child'. A child's preference is one factor, but not the deciding one. Judges often assess whether the child understands their choice or is being influenced. In many cases, older children—especially in early teens—have their views given more weight, but even younger voices can matter if they show maturity. The key takeaway: what matters is not just what the child wants, but why they want it.
conclusion: Do you think courts should give more weight to a child's preference once they turn a certain age, or should it always depend on maturity and circumstances of the case?
